coredata.dc:title: One-step sonochemical synthesis of versatile nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for sensitive detection of Fe2+ ions and temperature in vitro
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Meice
Zhou, Li
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be prepared by sonochemistry with low cost and high efficiency. However, ultrasonic synthesis of CQDs with responsive fluorescence towards environmental stimuli remains a big challenge. Herein, we report a simple one-step method to fabricate versatile nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) by ultrasonic treatment of dopamine in dimethylformamide for 8 h. The obtained NCQDs simultaneously possess superior water dispersibility and stability, bright and stable fluorescence against variations of pH and ionic strength, low cytotoxicity and high photostability. Moreover, the fluorescence of NCQDs showed sensitive quenching phenomenon towards Fe2+ ions both in water and in the interior of cancer cells. The emission intensity of NCQDs exhibited good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9987) with the concentration of Fe2+ ions in the range of 0–50 μM. The limit of detection can reach as high as 38 nM. Meanwhile, the NCQDs also exhibited temperature-dependent fluorescence with favorable linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9965) in the range of 283–358 K, and thus can server as a nanothermometer to sense temperature both in water and in cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots
Dopamine
Ultrasonic synthesis
Cell imaging
Detection of Fe2+ ions
Sensing of temperature

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of the NCQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the ultrasonic synthesis of fluorescent NCQDs from dopamine for cell imaging, sensing of Fe2+ ions, and optical thermometry.

(a) Representative AFM image of NCQDs. Inset of (a) is the corresponding height profile along the dashed line. (b) Representative TEM image of the NCQDs. Inset of (b) is the high-resolution TEM image of NCQDs. XPS survey (c), XPS C1s (d), XPS N1s (e), and FTIR (f) spectra of the NCQDs.

UV–vis absorption (a) and photoluminescence excitation (b) (λem = 434 nm) spectra of the aqueous dispersion of NCQDs. (c) Emission spectra of the aqueous dispersion of NCQDs upon excitation at different wavelengths. (d) A typical time-resolved fluorescence-decay curve of NCQDs (λex = 350 nm). pH (e) and NaCl concentration (f) dependent fluorescent intensity ratio (I/I0) of the NCQDs. I0 is the emission intensity of NCQDs dispersion in the absence of NaCl and at pH 7. I is the emission intensity of NCQDs dispersion at diverse pH values (c) or with diverse concentrations of NaCl (d).

CLSM fluorescence (a) and merged (b) images of HepG2 cells at 20 °C upon incubation with NCQDs (20 μg/mL) for 2 h. CLSM fluorescence (c) and merged (d) images of HepG2 cells without incubation with NCQDs. (e) Cell viability of HepG2 cells after incubation with NCQDs at diverse concentrations for 4 h and 24 h, respectively. (f) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) of NCQDs and fluorescein. I0 and I are the emission intensities of NCQDs and fluorescein without and with laser illumination for different time, respectively.

(a) Emission spectra of aqueous dispersion of NCQDs upon addition of diverse concentrations of Fe2+ ions (λex = 350 nm). (b) The dependence of I/I0 value on the concentration of Fe2+ ions. Inset: photographs of aqueous dispersion of NCQDs under 365 nm UV light before and after addition of Fe2+ ions (50 μM). (c) I/I0 value against the concentration of Fe2+ ions in the range of 0–50 μM. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of aqueous dispersion of NCQDs with diverse concentrations of Fe2+ ions. Inset: photographs of aqueous dispersion of NCQDs under daylight before and after addition of Fe2+ ions (50 μM). CLSM fluorescence (e) and bright (f) images of HepG2 cells at 20 °C after incubation with NCQDs (20 μg/mL) and Fe2+ ions (50 μM) for 2 h. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Photographs of aqueous dispersions of NCQDs under 365 nm UV light and daylight with 50 μM of diverse metal ions. (b) I/I0 value of aqueous dispersions of NCQDs with diverse metal ions at 50 μM each. (c) Selective fluorescence response of NCQDs dispersions towards 50 μM of Fe2+ ions (red bar), and interference of 50 μM of other metal ions with 50 μM of Fe2+ ions (blue bars). I0 is the emission intensity of NCQDs in the absence of metal ions. I is the emission intensity of NCQDs with various metal ions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Emission spectra of aqueous dispersion of NCQDs at diverse temperature. (b) Emission intensity of NCQDs dispersion against temperature variation. (c) Infrared thermal and fluorescent images of aqueous dispersions of NCQDs at 10 °C and 70 °C. (d) Emission intensity of the NCQDs dispersion as a function of heating-cooling cycles between 10 °C and 70 °C. CLSM fluorescence images of HepG2 cells at 20 °C (e) and 50 °C (d) and bight field image (f) after incubation with NCQDs (20 μg/mL) for 2 h.

head.ce:title: One-step sonochemical synthesis of versatile nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for sensitive detection of Fe2+ ions and temperature in vitro

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal and Materials (Ministry of Education), Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optical and Electronic Materials and Devices, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal and Materials (Ministry of Education)
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optical and Electronic Materials and Devices
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be prepared by sonochemistry with low cost and high efficiency. However, ultrasonic synthesis of CQDs with responsive fluorescence towards environmental stimuli remains a big challenge. Herein, we report a simple one-step method to fabricate versatile nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) by ultrasonic treatment of dopamine in dimethylformamide for 8 h. The obtained NCQDs simultaneously possess superior water dispersibility and stability, bright and stable fluorescence against variations of pH and ionic strength, low cytotoxicity and high photostability. Moreover, the fluorescence of NCQDs showed sensitive quenching phenomenon towards Fe2+ ions both in water and in the interior of cancer cells. The emission intensity of NCQDs exhibited good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9987) with the concentration of Fe2+ ions in the range of 0–50 μM. The limit of detection can reach as high as 38 nM. Meanwhile, the NCQDs also exhibited temperature-dependent fluorescence with favorable linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9965) in the range of 283–358 K, and thus can server as a nanothermometer to sense temperature both in water and in cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), combining superior optical property, high stability, versatile surface chemistry as well as favorable biocompatibility, hold great promise for applications in numerous fields, such as bioimaging, sensor, catalyst, and optoelectronic devices [1–10]. Several techniques, such as laser ablation, chemical oxidation, hydrothermal treatment, electrochemical oxidation, solar irradiation, and microwave treatment have been explored to synthesize fluorescent CQDs [11–16]. These methods, however, have raised concerns over expensive equipment, complex synthesis procedure, high temperature and high cost. Recently, the use of ultrasonic sonochemistry to fabricate CQDs has been reported [17–20]. Compared with other reported methods, ultrasonic synthesis of CQDs can be achieved at mild conditions with low cost and high efficiency. Nevertheless, for most of the reported CQDs, additional assistant (e.g., acid or alkali) is required during the ultrasonic process [17–19]. In addition, ultrasonic synthesis of CQDs with responsive fluorescence towards external stimuli has rarely been reported. To date, ultrasonic treatment of individual precursor to prepare fluorescent CQDs with responsive fluorescence remains a big challenge.

Iron element as a kind of essential element is very crucial for living systems. For instance, iron deficiency anemia has become one of the most common nutritional diseases in the world. In the past decade, the employment of fluorescent CQDs for the sensing of iron ions has been widely investigated based on the fluorescence quenching phenomenon upon addition of iron ions [21–32]. To our knowledge, however, most of the reported CQDs focused on the detection of Fe3+ ions [21–29]. So far, only three papers reported the sensing of Fe2+ ions by CQDs [30–32]. On the other hand, the Fe2+ ions are also essential ions for human and animal healthy [33,34]. For example, Fe2+ ions are not only crucial in the transport and storage of oxygen but also are key ions in energy metabolism. Therefore, using fluorescent CQDs to sensitive and selective detections of Fe2+ ions is still in its infancy. Since the fluorescence and surface properties of CQDs are highly dependent on the synthesis method and precursor, it is highly desired to explore viable method associated with appropriate precursor for synthesis of fluorescent CQDs with high sensitivity towards Fe2+ ions.

Recently, luminescence thermometry technique has captured intensive attention due to its potential for non-contact measurement of temperature in myriads of harsh situations [35]. Various luminescent materials such as semiconductor quantum dots, lanthanide doped inorganic materials and organic dyes, have been utilized as luminescence thermometry to determine temperature [35–38]. However, these luminescent materials suffer from the limitations of high toxicity, low sensitivity, and poor optical stability, which significantly restrict their practical applications. Based on their excellent physiochemical properties, the CQDs are expected to show good performance on sensing of temperature. However, the utilization of CQDs especially prepared by sonochemistry for optical thermometry has rarely been reported [39–41].

In this contribution, we report a simple one-step and inexpensive method to fabricate nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) by ultrasonic treatment of dopamine (Fig. 1 ). It is reported that the nitrogen-doping could endow the CQDs with versatile surface properties such as unique fluorescence responsiveness towards external stimuli [7,23,26]. Herein, the resulting NCQDs not only exhibited bright fluorescence but also showed high colloidal and optical stability. Preliminarily biological evaluation results demonstrate that the NCQDs possess low cytotoxicity and could emit bright blue fluorescence in the interior of cells. Moreover, the NCQDs exhibited responsive fluorescence behavior towards Fe2+ ions and environmental temperature. The employment of NCQDs as a fluorescent nanoprobe to sense Fe2+ ions and environmental temperature both in water and in cancer cells were studied in detail.

Dopamine hydrochloride (98%), dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.9%), dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO, 99.9%) methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT, 98%), Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (99.9%), FeSO4·7H2O (99.5%), NaCl (99.5%), AgNO3 (99.9%), Co(NO3)2·6H2O (99.9%), CdCl2 (99.9%), KBr (99.9%), Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (99.0%), FeCl3·6H2O (98.0%), CuCl2·2H2O (99.9%), Pb(NO3)2 (99.9%), CaCl2 (99.9%), and quinine sulfate were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used as received.

Atom force microscope (AFM) images of the NCQDs were measured by a NTMDT (Ntegra Prima) scanning probe microscope, operating at tapping mode. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the NCQDs were captured on a JEOL-2010 TEM with an accelerating voltage of 160 kV. FTIR 2500 spectrometer was used to record the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of NQCDs by using potassium bromide disk method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of NCQDs were recorded on Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD (Kratos Analytical Ltd) with mono Al Kα radiation (hν = 1487.71 eV). Varian Cary 100 spectrometer was employed to measure the emission and excitation spectra of NCQDs dispersions. UV-3600 UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) was utilized to determine the UV–vis absorption spectra of NCQDs dispersions. Photoluminescence quantum yield of the NCQDs dispersions was measured by utilizing quinine sulfate in H2SO4 (0.1 M) as reference (QY = 55%). Cell imaging and photostability measurements were conducted on a Zeiss LSM 510 confocal laser-scanning microscopy (CLSM). The cytotoxicity of the NCQDs was examined by standard MTT assay using human hepatoblastoma (HepG2) cells as model according to our previous reports [39,40]. The HepG2 cells were purchased from Fenghui Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Changsha, China).

Typically, 300 mg of dopamine hydrochloride was dissolved in 150 mL of DMF. Ultrasonic irradiation of the mixture was conducted by an ultrasonic homogenizer (YMNL, 1000Y) equipped with a tip for 8 h in an open glass vessel. An ice bath was employed to cool the mixture. The homogenizer worked in a pulsed mode (600 W, 20 kHz) with a frequency of 1 s. The final mixture was centrifuged to remove big particles. The obtained supernatant was then dialyzed against deionized water (MW cutoff 500 Da) to afford aqueous dispersion of dopamine-derived NCQDs.

In a typical procedure, aqueous dispersions of NCQDs and Fe2+ ions (or other metal ions) with known volume and concentration were mixed in a cuvette (3.5 mL). After shaking the mixture for 1 min, the fluorescent spectrum of mixture was recorded (λex = 350 nm).

The cell imaging experiments were performed according to our previous reports [42,43]. Typically, 0.5 mL of NCQDs dispersion (20 μg/mL) was incubated with HepG2 cancer cells at 37 °C for 4 h. The cells were then washed repeatedly with PBS buffer to remove the excess NCQDs. After that, the cells were fixed by 75% ethanol solution and followed by washing with PBS buffer. The HepG2 cancer cells were imaged by the CLSM and a heater was used to tune the temperature of sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: After ultrasound irradiation of dopamine in DMF for 8 h, the liquid mixture turned from colorless to dark brown due to the formation of NCQDs. It is reported that the cavitation bubbles produced in liquid solution during sonication can generate hot spot with temperature as high as several thousand degrees [44]. Accordingly, the formation of NCQDs is possibly attributed to the pyrolysis of dopamine around the hot spot. Similarly, ultrasonic treatment of various organic molecules to afford graphitic carbon materials have been widely reported [45–47]. The resulting NCQDs showed high colloidal stability and no detectable precipitates could be seen even storage in water for three months (Fig. S1 in the Supplementary material).

After ultrasound irradiation of dopamine in DMF for 8 h, the liquid mixture turned from colorless to dark brown due to the formation of NCQDs. It is reported that the cavitation bubbles produced in liquid solution during sonication can generate hot spot with temperature as high as several thousand degrees [44]. Accordingly, the formation of NCQDs is possibly attributed to the pyrolysis of dopamine around the hot spot. Similarly, ultrasonic treatment of various organic molecules to afford graphitic carbon materials have been widely reported [45–47]. The resulting NCQDs showed high colloidal stability and no detectable precipitates could be seen even storage in water for three months (Fig. S1 in the Supplementary material).

Fig. 2a shows a representative atom force microscope (AFM) image of product obtained from irradiated dopamine. The AFM image associated with height analysis result indicates that the height distribution of NCQDs is between 2.8 and 4.1 nm (Fig. 2a). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image (Fig. 2b) indicates the diameter of 2.5–5.5 nm, which is in accordance with the AFM result. In addition, no obvious lattice fringe can be seen in the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of NCQDs (Fig. 2b, inset), implying their amorphous nature. To further probe the structure and surface information of NCQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy measurements were conducted. The XPS survey spectrum presents three obvious bands centered at 284.2 eV, 399.2 eV, and 531.5 eV, which are assigned to the C1s, N1s, and O1s signal, respectively (Fig. 2c). The high resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2d demonstrates that the NCQDs contain four kinds of carbon with peaks at 284.1 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.6 eV, and 288.1 eV, which are corresponding to CCC, CN, CO and CO/CN bonds, respectively [48,49]. In addition, the N1s spectrum of NCQDs indicates the presence of pyridine-like N (399.3 eV), pyrrolic N (400.1 eV) and NH (401.0 eV) (Fig. 2e) [49]. Consistent with the XPS results, the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2f) of NCQDs also confirms the existence of OH, NH, CH, CN, CC, and CO groups on the surface of NCQDs. The superior aqueous dispersibility and stability of NCQDs should be attributed to the existence of myriads of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of NCQDs [17,39,48].

The optical properties of aqueous dispersion of NCQDs were systematically studied. As shown in Fig. 3a, the NCQDs exhibit two obvious absorption bands centered at 412 nm and 284 nm, and a weak shoulder band centered at 323 nm. The peak at 412 nm is possibly ascribed to the trapping of surface excited state energy of NCQDs. The peaks at 323 nm and 284 nm are possibly caused by the n − π* transition of CO bonds and the π − π* transition of CC/CN bonds, respectively [50]. It is worth noting that no obvious change can be observed from the absorption spectrum of NCQDs before and after storage in water for three months, further demonstrated the superior colloidal stability of NCQDs in water (Fig. S1). Analogous to previously reported NCQDs [49–51], the obtained NCQDs also showed excitation-dependent fluorescence (Fig. 3c). With increasing the excitation wavelength, the associated emission peaks gradually shifted to long wavelength. The maximum emission peak with fluorescence quantum yield of 3.6% appears at 434 nm upon 350 nm excitation. This result agrees well with their excitation spectrum, in which an obvious excitation peak at 350 nm could be observed (Fig. 3b). The average fluorescence lifetime (τav) of NCQDs was determined to be 3.02 ns (Fig. 3d), which compares favorably to other reported CQDs [52,53]. In addition, the influences of pH and electrolyte on fluorescent properties of NCQDs were also studied. As depicted in Fig. 3e and f, the NCQDs present stable fluorescence against the change of solution pH and the increase concentration of NaCl electrolyte, suggesting their suitability to be utilized in harsh environment.

The optical properties of aqueous dispersion of NCQDs were systematically studied. As shown in Fig. 3a, the NCQDs exhibit two obvious absorption bands centered at 412 nm and 284 nm, and a weak shoulder band centered at 323 nm. The peak at 412 nm is possibly ascribed to the trapping of surface excited state energy of NCQDs. The peaks at 323 nm and 284 nm are possibly caused by the n − π* transition of CO bonds and the π − π* transition of CC/CN bonds, respectively [50]. It is worth noting that no obvious change can be observed from the absorption spectrum of NCQDs before and after storage in water for three months, further demonstrated the superior colloidal stability of NCQDs in water (Fig. S1). Analogous to previously reported NCQDs [49–51], the obtained NCQDs also showed excitation-dependent fluorescence (Fig. 3c). With increasing the excitation wavelength, the associated emission peaks gradually shifted to long wavelength. The maximum emission peak with fluorescence quantum yield of 3.6% appears at 434 nm upon 350 nm excitation. This result agrees well with their excitation spectrum, in which an obvious excitation peak at 350 nm could be observed (Fig. 3b). The average fluorescence lifetime (τav) of NCQDs was determined to be 3.02 ns (Fig. 3d), which compares favorably to other reported CQDs [52,53]. In addition, the influences of pH and electrolyte on fluorescent properties of NCQDs were also studied. As depicted in Fig. 3e and f, the NCQDs present stable fluorescence against the change of solution pH and the increase concentration of NaCl electrolyte, suggesting their suitability to be utilized in harsh environment.

Because of their bright fluorescence and superior aqueous dispersibility and stability, the utilization of NCQDs as a fluorescent nanoprobe for cell imaging was investigated. Human hepatoblastoma (HepG2) cell was chosen as a representative cancer cell. After incubation with NCQDs dispersion for 2 h, the HepG2 cells were imaged by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). As depicted in Fig. 4a and b, bright blue fluorescence can be seen in the interior of HepG2 cells upon laser excitation at 364 nm, indicating that the NCQDs had been internalized by HepG2 cancer cells. It is worth noting that no fluorescence could be observed for the HepG2 cells without adding NCQDs (Fig. 4c and d). On the other hand, the cytotoxicity of NCQDs was examined using MTT viability assay by choosing HepG2 cancer cells as a representative. Within the tested period (4 h and 24 h), the cell viabilities of HepG2 cells were close to 100% when the concentration of NCQDs below 100 μg/mL and still near to 90% even with high concentration of NCQDs (e.g., 300 μg/mL) (Fig. 4e), demonstrating the low cytotoxicity of NCQDs. Meanwhile, the photostability of NCQDs was examined by continuous laser illumination for 10 min. For comparison, the photostability of fluorescein was also investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 4f, the NCQDs exhibited slight (<13%) fluorescence decline, whereas the emission intensity of fluorescein declined by 83%. Therefore, the fluorescent NCQDs hold great promise as a nanoprobe for cell imaging because of their strong fluorescence, low toxicity and high photostability.

Notably, the fluorescent intensity of aqueous dispersion of NCQDs decline gradually upon addition of Fe2+ ions (Fig. 5a), suggesting that the fluorescence of NCQDs is sensitive to the Fe2+ ions. As presented in the inset of Fig. 5b, the strong blue fluorescence of NCQDs dispersion became very weak after adding Fe2+ ions. With the concentration of Fe2+ ions in the range of 0–50 μM, the variation of emission intensity (I/I0 value) exhibited good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9987) (Fig. 5c). The sensing limit was measured to be 38 nM, which is more sensitive than other reported fluorescent probes [32,54]. In addition, UV–vis absorption spectra of NCQDs dispersion before and after addition of Fe2+ ions were determined. As exhibited in Fig. 5d, with increasing the concentration of Fe2+ ions, the absorption peak at 412 nm gradually shifted to 404 nm and the intensity of absorption peak at 284 nm declined significantly. Furthermore, the aqueous dispersion of NCQDs with pale yellow color became dark brown after addition of Fe2+ ions, which should be caused by the strong coordination interactions between the surface functional groups of NCQDs and Fe2+ ions. In addition, the sensing of Fe2+ ions in cancer cells was also studied. As can be observed in Fig. 5e and f, the HepG2 cells after incubation of NCQDs followed by addition of 50 μM of Fe2+ ions exhibited much weaker fluorescence than that of without addition of Fe2+ ions (Fig. 4a and b), implying that the NCQDs can be utilized as a fluorescent nanoprobe to detect Fe2+ ions in cells.

To further study the selectivity of NCQDs towards Fe2+ ions, other common metal ions including Na+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Ag+, Co2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, and Ni2+ ions were respectively mixed with aqueous dispersion of NCQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 6a, the blue fluorescence of NCQDs was almost completely quenched by 50 μM of Fe2+ ions. In contrast, other metal ions only presented slight (e.g., Fe3+, Pb2+, and Cu2+) or negligible (e.g., Na+, K+, and Ca2+) quenching effect. Consistent with the photographs, the I/I0 results (Fig. 6b) further demonstrate that the fluorescence of NCQDs can be selectively quenched by Fe2+ ions. Meanwhile, the influence of coexisting metal ions on the selective detection of Fe2+ ions was also studied. The emission intensities of NCQDs dispersions at 434 nm after addition of Fe2+ ions (50 μM) alone and the mixture of Fe2+ ions (50 μM) and other metal ions (50 μM) were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 6c, other coexisting metal ions (e.g., Cu2+, Fe3+, and Zn2+) only show slight quenching efficacy on the fluorescence of NCQDs. Therefore, the fluorescent NCQDs can serve as a promising nanoprobe for detection of Fe2+ ions due to their high sensitivity and selectivity. Although the accurate quenching mechanism is still unclear, the superior fluorescent sensitivity and selectivity of NCQDs towards Fe2+ ions is probably attributed to the strong coordination interactions between the surface functional groups of NCQDs and the Fe2+ ions based on the above optical phenomenon and results. The strong coordination interactions can interrupt the radiative transition of NCQDs and thereby resulting in the fluorescence quenching of NCQDs.

In addition to Fe2+ ions, the NCQDs also exhibited responsive fluorescence towards external temperature. As presented in Fig. 7a, the emission intensity of aqueous dispersion of NCQDs decreased gradually with raising temperature. The curve of emission intensity versus external temperature was plotted (Fig. 7b). Obviously, the emission intensity showed linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9965) against external temperature. The emission intensity of NCQDs decreased by 41.7% within 75 °C, corresponding to a sensitivity of 0.55%/°C. As can be seen in Fig. 7c, the aqueous dispersion of NCQDs can emit bright blue fluorescence at 10 °C under 365 nm UV light, while only weak blue fluorescence could be seen at 70 °C. Moreover, the temperature-responsive behavior of NCQDs is highly repeatable as demonstrated by five consecutive heating-cooling cycles between 10 and 70 °C (Fig. 7d). In addition, the utilization of NCQDs as a fluorescent nanothermometer to sense intracellular temperature was also investigated. HepG2 cancer cell was chosen as a representative system to stain with NCQDs and then the cells at diverse temperatures were imaged by CLSM (Fig. 7e–g). Clearly, the cytoplasm of HepG2 cells can emit bright fluorescence at 20 °C (Fig. 7e). Upon raising temperature to 50 °C, the blue fluorescence in cancer cells became very weak (Fig. 7f). This phenomenon is well in accordance with the variations observed from the emission spectra (Fig. 7a). Based on the above results, the NCQDs hold great promise to serve as an optical thermometry with high accuracy and reproducibility.

In conclusion, we have demonstrated a simple one-step method to fabricate versatile NCQDs by ultrasonic irradiation of dopamine in DMF. The resulted NCQDs simultaneously showed bright fluorescence, superior aqueous dispersibility and stability, favorable optical stability, and low cytotoxicity. The fluorescence of NCQDs presented sensitive and selective quenching performance towards Fe2+ ions. In addition, the NCQDs also showed repeatable temperature-dependent fluorescence. As the first example to ultrasonic synthesis of NCQDs with responsive fluorescence, this study not only exploits a viable approach to prepare responsive NCQDs but also provides new fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of Fe3+ ions and temperature.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
UV–vis absorption spectra of the aqueous dispersions of freshly prepared NCQDs and after storage for three months. Insets: photographs of the aqueous dispersions of freshly prepared NCQDs and after storage for three months.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2019.03.109.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51663007), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2017GXNSFFA198002), Bagui Young Scholar Program of Guangxi Province, and the Project of Thousand Outstanding Young Teachers' Training in Higher Education Institutions of Guangxi.
